---
title: "Workflow Roadmap"
---

## The end-to-end workflow

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A[Question and scope] --> B[Access and infrastructure]
  B --> C[Project scaffold]
  C --> D[Data intake and documentation]
  D --> E[Reusable scripts or pipeline]
  E --> F[Exploration and notebooks]
  F --> G[Review and communication]
  F --> E
  G --> F
  G --> H[Archive and handoff]

```

The point of the workflow is not bureaucracy. The point is to make a project understandable and rerunnable at every stage.

### 1. Question and scope

These are the foundational elements that you may discuss with
your PI, your colleagues, and your collaborators. You may start 
by simply writing down things like:

- the scientific question,
- the unit of analysis,
- the key inputs,
- the expected outputs,
- and the likely compute or security constraints.

Much of this work will exist in Google Drive as meeting notes,
lecture notes, and other communications. This is where you establish
your motivation and high-level scientific field of interest.

### 2. Access and infrastructure

Before writing any code, decide where the work belongs:

- publicly available data and identifiable data at or below security level 2 is appropriate for the standard FASRC environment, sometimes called CANNON; the vast majority of our lab work will fit this description;
- restricted data at security level 3 or above may require [FASSE](https://docs.rc.fas.harvard.edu/kb/fasse/?seq_no=2) and a secure compute environment.

Additionally, think about which tools are best for the job. In this lab, we use R and Python,
but the workflow principles apply to either and any other language you may use. 

## 3. Project scaffold

#todo create template repo and link here
Set up the repository immediately. A good starting point is:

```text
project-name/
├── README.md
├── .gitignore
├── data/
├── data-raw/
├── code/
├── notebooks/
├── reports/
├── figures/
├── results/
└── renv.lock or environment.yml
```

The NSAPH handbook recommends a very similar repository layout because it separates code, figures, reports, results, and data from the start.[^nsaph-fasse]

## 4. Data intake and documentation

Before analysis scales up, document:

- where the authoritative data lives,
- who controls access,
- whether you should symlink instead of copy,
- the update cadence,
- key identifiers,
- and what cannot leave the secure environment.

If a dataset deserves an analysis, it deserves a data dictionary.

## 5. Exploration and notebooks

Do early exploration in notebooks or scripts that explain:

- what you are testing,
- what assumptions you are making,
- what looks wrong,
- and what should happen next.

R for Data Science treats communication as part of the data science loop, not a final wrapper placed on top of "real" analysis. That is the right model for lab work too.[^r4ds-communicate]

## 6. Reusable scripts or pipeline

Once a workflow stabilizes, move repeated logic out of exploratory notebooks and into:

- scripts,
- functions,
- parameterized reports,
- or a pipeline tool such as `targets`, `make`, or `snakemake`.

The trigger is simple: if you expect to rerun it, scale it, or hand it off, it should not live only in an exploratory notebook cell.

## 7. Review and communication

Every project should be able to produce:

1. a short `README`,
2. one current notebook or report that explains the analysis status,
3. a reproducible path from data inputs to results,
4. and a Git history that reflects real milestones.

This makes supervisor meetings, methods review, and cross-training materially easier.

## 8. Archive and handoff

Before a paper, graduation, or staff transition:

- confirm the repo can be cloned and run from scratch,
- confirm restricted data are not in Git,
- confirm documentation identifies canonical data locations,
- render a final report or project website,
- and record ownership for future maintenance.

## Minimal viable workflow

If you are overwhelmed, do these five things first:

1. Work on the correct cluster.
2. Put the analysis in Git.
3. Use relative paths.
4. Keep one notebook or report up to date.
5. Write enough documentation that another lab member could restart the project next week.

## Further reading

- [R for Data Science (2e): Workflow: scripts and projects](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/workflow-scripts)
- [R for Data Science (2e): Communicate](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/communicate)
- [NSAPH Handbook: How to work on FASSE](https://nsaph.github.io/handbook/fasse.html)
- [NSAPH Handbook: Workflow Documentation](https://nsaph.github.io/handbook/docs.html)

[^nsaph-fasse]: NSAPH Handbook, [*How to work on FASSE*](https://nsaph.github.io/handbook/fasse.html).
[^r4ds-communicate]: Hadley Wickham, Mine Cetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund, [*R for Data Science (2e): Communicate*](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/communicate).
